topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white of the lab report seemed to mock the muted colors of my apartment. I stared at the printout, the ink blurring slightly under the harsh overhead light. My hands, usually steady, trembled as I traced the lines of the genetic markers. It was a confirmation, undeniable and utterly crushing. I felt a sudden, heavy weight settle in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. Who was I, really? A collection of borrowed traits, a carefully constructed narrative built on someone else's blueprint. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound a small, pathetic rustle, and shoved it deep into a drawer, hoping the paper could somehow disappear into the wood.

[story 2]

I kept tracing the pattern on the screen, my gaze fixed on the percentages. Each number felt like a tiny shard of glass scraping against my insides. A slow, dull ache radiated from my shoulders, pulling my posture down. It wasn't anger, not exactly; it was more like a profound emptiness, as if a vital core had been unexpectedly hollowed out. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under the pressure, and let out a sound that was more of a weary sigh than an actual noise. "This changes everything," I murmured to the empty room, the words tasting like ash. I didn't move to clean up the evidence; I just sat there, feeling profoundly disconnected from the world around me.

[story 3]

The sunlight filtering through the dusty blinds did little to warm the room. I picked up the envelope, the paper feeling thin and fragile in my palm. Reading the names—the names that weren't mine—sent a cold tremor through my limbs. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles protesting. I didn't cry. Instead, a strange stillness overtook me, a sort of paralysis where movement felt exhausting. I walked to the window, looking out at the street below, watching the hurried lives unfold. Every passing car seemed vibrant, full of purpose, while I felt like a shadow lingering at the edge of existence, detached and profoundly insignificant.
